text: Today we'll be viewing a video of a resection of a Meckel's diverticulum. In this particular case, the young lady presented with nonspecific abdominal pain in her lower pelvis. She previously had a cholecystectomy performed.
The surgeon examines the small bowel and identifies a large Meckel's diverticulum located approximately two feet from the ileocecal valve.

The surgeon identifies the Meckel's diverticulum and prepares to use a laparoscopic GIE stapler for transection. The anterior lumen of the small bowel is assessed to ensure adequate space to prevent obstruction.

The surgeon evaluates the anterior and posterior aspects of the small bowel resection site. The posterior lumen appears narrowed, prompting a readjustment to ensure adequate space and prevent obstruction.
The surgeon uses a power GIE stapler to transect the Meckel's diverticulum. The lumen is checked for adequacy before firing the stapler, ensuring a smooth transection without complications.
The surgeon inspects the staple line on the small bowel, confirming that the bowel is completely intact with no visible areas of concern.
The surgeon introduces an endocatch bag into the surgical field, places the resected specimen inside, and successfully removes it from the body without complications.
